body:
# Release Notes — v1.19.2 ## Fixes ### Part-time and ephemeral instances no longer show $0 savings on stop/idle recommendations Stop, deallocate, and pause recommendations sized the compute cost as if every box ran 24×7 (`HourlyRate × 730h`). For a part-time or ephemeral instance the actual billed amount is below that full-month rack rate, so the projected post-action cost went *negative* and the saving collapsed to **null / $0** — silently hiding a real opportunity. The engine now sizes the power-off saving against the hours the instance **actually ran** (`HourlyRate × uptime% × 730h`), using the same authoritative uptime signal the schedule and commitment rules already read. When uptime is unknown the conservative 24×7 figure is kept, so nothing that fired before stops firing. ### S3 "no lifecycle policy" cold-tier recommendation never fired The cold-tier saving recommendation for S3 buckets without a lifecycle policy (RC-072) was silently dropping for every bucket. The discoverer emits `lifecycle_policy_present`, but the rule read a differently-named `lifecycle_policy` key — the two never matched, so the rule always saw "has lifecycle policy = false… wait, true" and skipped. (The rule's own unit tests keyed on the wrong name too, which masked the drift.) The key mismatch is fixed at the data boundary, preserving the absent / false / true distinction so a missing attribute is never mistaken for "no policy". S3 tier recommendations now surface on the next engine run. ### Overview credited $0 when a priced saving existed on the same recommendation When a recommendation's frozen default option pointed at a $0 hygiene action (e.g. *add a tag*) while a real money-saving option existed on the same card (e.g. *delete*, saving $16.20), the **overview total still read $0** for that recommendation. The summary now credits the best **priced, non-removal** option instead of blindly trusting the $0 default — so the headline savings number reflects what you could actually save. Removal/delete options are deliberately excluded from this promotion so a hygiene recommendation never gets credited with a data-loss action's savings. The recommendation's displayed default option is unchanged. --- ## Enhancements ### Honest delete pricing for already-stopped instances A "delete this idle resource" recommendation on a **currently-stopped** instance used to be either a phantom full-24×7 figure (v1) or a flat $0 (v2). It's now priced honestly: **attached-storage cost that still bills + compute for the hours the box actually ran**. If the instance has been busy in the last 30 days (uptime > 5%) it's treated as a schedule/idle candidate, not a delete, and shows no fabricated number; figures below a $1/mo floor or with missing inputs return no basis rather than a guess. ### Wider rightsizing coverage — Graviton generation upgrades and single-AZ resilience gaps - **Generation / Graviton** — EC2, RDS, and ElastiCache are now evaluated for arm64 (Graviton) generation upgrades, using an architecture classifier rather than a brittle instance-family name list. EC2 emits an actionable migrate-to-arm64 recommendation today; RDS and ElastiCache fire as advisories until their pricing levers land. - **Replication / Multi-AZ** — single-AZ (`ha=false`) datastores running in **production** now surface a resilience-gap advisory, the direction that was previously missing. - **GCP committed-use discounts** — the detector and lever are wired and correct; the remaining gap is purely discovery-side (GCP cost must be ingested before purchase type is known), and the rule correctly stays quiet until then. Pinned with tests so it doesn't regress. --- ## Technical details
Uptime-aware power-off cost — threading the authoritative uptime signal into the projector The engine threads the authoritative `uptime_pct` signal (metric snapshot → aggregator attribute fallback, the same source the commitment/schedule rules read) into `projector.Input.MeasuredUptimePct`. The power-off case (stop / deallocate / pause) sizes compute as `HourlyRate × (uptimePct/100 × 730)` instead of the hardcoded `HourlyRate × 730`, bounded by the observed monthly. **Unknown uptime (0) keeps the conservative 730 behaviour**, so existing guards are unchanged. The metrics→engine import cycle is avoided via a pinned local `metricKeyUptimePct` constant, drift-guarded by tests on both sides.
Honest delete pricing for stopped compute — ParentUID storage correlation For a currently-stopped box: `saving = attachedStorageMonthlyUSD + HourlyRate × (uptimePct/100 × 730)` — the EBS residual that still bills plus the compute for hours actually run. Returns a **null basis** (never fabricated) when 30-day uptime **> 5%**, when inputs are missing, or below the **$1/mo** floor. The attached-storage residual is sourced at the service layer via **ParentUID** correlation (`costs.fetcher`); the delete capability itself is already emitted by the orphan rule. The stopped-delete gate is keyed on **known** uptime, and the `a1` family is on the Graviton allowlist. End-to-end dollar figures still depend on the discoverer setting `ParentUID` on volumes and the aggregator exposing per-volume cost (the pricing logic itself is unit-tested).
Best-priced summary credit The summary credits `BestPricedSavingsUSD`: a priced default is used as-is; a $0 / upsize default is promoted to the highest-saving **Available, non-removal** sibling on the same recommendation. Removals are excluded so a hygiene recommendation whose only priced sibling is a delete stays $0 — no data-loss footgun. The displayed default option is unchanged, and the stale-default healer (`RecRefresher` → `RefreshOpenRecs`) was already live and tested.
S3 tier key-drift fix Fixed at the lister boundary: alias `lifecycle_policy_present` → `lifecycle_policy` **only when present**, preserving the three-valued absent / false / true contract (an absent attribute must not synthesize a `false`). +4 boundary tests. The `intelligent_tiering` tier path still has no discoverer source attribute and needs a discoverer change to emit the boolean (follow-up).
Generation / replication coverage - **generation/Graviton** — EC2/RDS/ElastiCache added to applicability with an arm64 detector (architecture classifier, not a substring family list). EC2 materialises via the existing `aws-ec2-migrate-arch-arm64` lever; RDS/ElastiCache fire advisory until a generation-axis lever is added under `providers/`. - **replication/Multi-AZ** — added the missing resilience-gap direction (single-AZ / `ha=false` on prod) — $0 posture, advisory. - **commitment/GCP-CUD** — detector + lever already exist and are wired; the gap is discovery-side (`purchase_type_known=false` when GCP cost isn't ingested) and the rule correctly suppresses. Pinned with tests documenting the dependency.
Test-only — tag-trust invariant Pins that a customer env tag (a convention the cloud doesn't enforce) is **never the sole trigger** for a cost action: a dev-tagged box with no usage signal, and dev-tag + uptime but no CPU, both correctly produce no schedule recommendation. Codifies that v2's tag-vs-authoritative-fact discipline must not regress to v1's tag-only firing.
Scope, build, and deploy - Cherry-pick of #1695 + #1698 onto `v1.19.1` (= current `main`) — **only the 7 recommender commits**, isolated from everything else merged to `development` since v1.19.1 (ClickHouse #1655, remediation #1608, etc.), so v1.19.2 ships nothing but the v2 pricing/coverage fixes. - **18 files changed, all under `backend/recommender/internal/v2/`** — no other service touched. - **Zero new DB migrations** (the `resource_json` migration is already in v1.19.1 via #1686). - Builds clean; full `./internal/v2/...` suite green; gofmt + vet clean. - `deploy.yml` ships prod (`XYZ-beta`) on **`release: published`** and diffs the tag vs the previous release — publishing `v1.19.2` rebuilds + redeploys **only the recommender**. Merge this to `main`, publish the `v1.19.2` release to deploy, then re-run the engine to apply the new pricing/coverage to live recommendations.
